skill.name: metabolomics-workbench-database
skill.description: Access NIH Metabolomics Workbench REST API documentation for studies, compounds, RefMet names, m/z searches, and gene or protein metadata.
skill.summary: Guide metabolomics data queries against the Metabolomics Workbench REST API.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review shows the findings are documentation false positives or expected public API examples. The skill contains Markdown REST examples for Metabolomics Workbench, not executable malware, prompt injection, credential theft, or hidden exfiltration. Low residual risk remains because users may run network requests and one example writes a downloaded PNG file.

low_findings.title: Expected Public API Network Access
low_findings.description: The skill documents HTTP requests to the official Metabolomics Workbench REST API. This is expected for the skill purpose, and no credential exfiltration or hidden endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain direct HTTP examples to metabolomicsworkbench.org, which matches the declared purpose. The reviewed examples do not include secrets, credential collection, or unrelated destinations.

low_findings.title: Example File Write for Downloaded Structure Image
low_findings.description: One Python workflow writes a downloaded compound PNG to structure.png. This is a local example artifact, not hidden persistence or arbitrary filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write target is a fixed example filename and the content comes from the documented structure-image endpoint. There is no evidence of reading local files or writing sensitive paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Many external-command alerts come from Markdown code fences and inline code spans, not Ruby backtick execution. Curl examples are user-facing documentation and are not automatically executed by the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged characters appear in Markdown fences or inline API terms. There is no Ruby source file, executable script, dynamic command construction, or automatic command invocation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Biomedical Terms Misclassified as Sensitive or Cryptographic Indicators
dangerous_patterns.description: The SAM, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are caused by words such as SAMPLE_SOURCE, MS, M-H, exactmass, and sequence fields in API documentation. No Windows SAM database access, cryptography, or host reconnaissance intent was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are chemical, mass-spectrometry, or API field names in context. I found no code that accesses system databases, performs cryptography, or enumerates host information.

content.value_statement: Metabolomics researchers need reliable access to public study, metabolite, and m/z data. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Metabolomics Workbench REST queries.
actual_capabilities: Explains Metabolomics Workbench REST endpoint structure and output formats.
Helps query compound data by registry number, PubChem CID, InChI Key, KEGG ID, or formula.
Guides study searches by study ID, metabolite, investigator, institute, species, disease, or sample source.
Supports RefMet name standardization, classification lookup, formula search, and exact mass lookup.
Provides m/z search patterns for adduct-based mass spectrometry compound identification.
Documents gene and protein metadata queries through the Metabolome Gene/Protein database.
limitations: It depends on the external Metabolomics Workbench REST API being available.
It does not validate scientific conclusions or replace expert metabolomics review.
Large API responses may need separate parsing, caching, or downstream analysis tools.
The included examples are documentation patterns, not a packaged client library.

use_cases.title: Find public studies for a metabolite
use_cases.description: Standardize a metabolite name with RefMet, then identify matching studies and retrieve study summaries.

use_cases.title: Annotate mass spectrometry features
use_cases.description: Search candidate compounds by m/z value, adduct type, database, and tolerance.

use_cases.title: Build reproducible data pulls
use_cases.description: Map REST endpoint patterns into scripts that collect compounds, studies, and gene or protein metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Find metabolite studies
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Metabolomics Workbench skill to find public studies related to glucose. Standardize the name first and summarize the study search path.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve compound details
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to query Metabolomics Workbench for compound details, identifiers, and structure information using PubChem CID 5281365.

prompt_templates.title: Search m/z candidates
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Metabolomics Workbench m/z search plan for 180.0634 with M+H adduct and high-resolution tolerance.

prompt_templates.title: Design a study retrieval workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reproducible workflow to find human blood diabetes LC-MS studies, retrieve summaries, and identify measured metabolites.

output_examples.output: The skill first standardizes Tyrosine through RefMet, then uses the study RefMet-name endpoint to retrieve matching study summaries.

output_examples.input: Identify candidates for m/z 635.52 in positive mode.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a Moverz search against the selected database with M+H adduct and a tolerance suited to the instrument resolution.
The skill points to gene-symbol and protein endpoints that return gene metadata, protein identifiers, sequence length, and related annotations.

best_practices: Use RefMet standardization before comparing metabolite names across studies.
Choose adduct type and tolerance values that match the mass spectrometry method.
Cache repeated reference lookups when running large study or compound searches.
anti_patterns: Do not treat candidate m/z matches as confirmed compound identities without validation.
Do not assume every API response has the same fields across resource types.
Do not run bulk API collection without delays and local caching.

faq.question: Does this skill include a runnable API client?
faq.answer: No. It provides endpoint guidance, examples, and workflows for the public REST API.

faq.question: Can it access private Metabolomics Workbench data?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows focus on publicly available endpoints and public study data.

faq.question: Can it standardize metabolite names?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to use RefMet match and lookup endpoints for standardized nomenclature.

faq.question: Does it support mass spectrometry annotation?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents Moverz searches by database, mass, adduct, tolerance, and output format.

faq.question: Can it retrieve molecular structures?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents compound endpoints for MOL files and PNG structure images.

faq.question: Is network access required?
faq.answer: Yes. Real queries require outbound requests to the Metabolomics Workbench website.
